title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman and Sam Riley walk through Toby Sumpter's eight tenets of 'smashmouth incrementalism' point-by-point, arguing that his own admissions, his theology of gradual reform, and his analogies to divorce, polygamy, and Cain collapse under scrutiny when applied to the immediate abolition of abortion. The article frames abolitionism as immediate obedience to Christ and incremental legislation as idolatrous compromise that sustains the very politicians who keep abortion legal.
audiences: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
Pro-life legislators weighing whether to support heartbeat or gestational-limit bills
Christians new to the abolitionism vs. incrementalism debate
Abolitionist activists preparing to engage smashmouth-incrementalist arguments
Skeptics who think abolitionists are merely splitting hairs over tactics

questions.question: Why do the authors say Sumpter's first tenet, taken at face value, would actually end the debate in abolitionism's favor?
questions.audience: Christians new to the abolitionism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: Because Tenet 1 condemns 'all efforts to stymy, stonewall, ignore, or bury' abolition legislation, and incremental bills are precisely what gives pro-life politicians cover to bury abolition bills. If Sumpter actually meant what he wrote, he would have to repudiate the incremental schemes he otherwise defends.
questions.quote: Second, to condemn “all efforts to stymy, stonewall, ignore, or bury such legislative measures” is to condemn incrementalism. Inherent to incrementalism is the promotion of legislation that is used as a substitute for an abolition bill. The politicians that stonewall abolition bills would not be able to get away with it were it not for incrementalist schemes.

questions.question: What role do the authors say incremental legislation plays in keeping abortion legal?
questions.audience: Pro-life legislators weighing whether to support heartbeat or gestational-limit bills
questions.answer: It functions as the political lifeblood that protects pro-life politicians and lobbyists from accountability. Cut off the supply of incremental bills and those politicians lose their excuse and must either abolish abortion or lose their seats.
questions.quote: **Incremental legislation is the lifeblood of the pro-life politicians and lobbyists who are keeping abortion legal.** When we cut off the stream of incremental bills by popularizing the repudiation of them, these politicians will have no choice but to abolish abortion or lose their seats.

questions.question: How does the article use Sumpter's own exchange with Jeff Durbin to argue that the heartbeat-bill debate is already over?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
questions.answer: Sumpter conceded on-air that for someone who knows better and has the option to abolish abortion, supporting a heartbeat bill is idolatry and compromise. Once that admission is made, the authors argue, the bill is morally off the table, because idolatry is treason against Christ.
questions.quote:
> Jeff: “Ok, hold on real fast. Just to make sure it’s clear: you’re saying a heartbeat bill is idolatry and compromise?”
>
> Toby: “For the one who knows better and who has the option [to abolish abortion].”

This is another debate-ending admission from Sumpter. The minute he concedes that it’s idolatry, it’s off the table. It is treason against our King.

questions.question: Why do the authors reject Sumpter's appeal to gospel-as-leaven and gradual sanctification as a defense of incremental abortion bills?
questions.audience: Christians new to the abolitionism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: They concede that gradual reformation can result from prophetic preaching, but insist the prophets' call itself was always for immediate repentance and never celebrated unjust, idolatrous laws as partial wins. Sumpter conflates God's gradual fruit with man's compromised standard.
questions.quote: Yes, gradual reformation sometimes results from a prophetic call to repentance. But the call to repentance is ALWAYS for immediate repentance. The prophets never called for or celebrated unjust, idolatrous reforms as Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic do. The prophets lifted high the uncompromising standard that God had them raise and no other.

questions.question: How does the article respond to Sumpter's appeal to God's mercy on Cain as a model for incremental justice?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
questions.answer: It rejects the analogy outright: God sparing Cain was an act of divine mercy, not God incrementally conforming to His own standard, and it provides no warrant for human legislatures to pass laws that protect some children while licensing the murder of others.
questions.quote: There is a lot to respond to here, most of it being — again — bad theology. God choosing to show Cain mercy has absolutely nothing to do with supporting legislation that discriminates against some children. God not striking Cain dead was not an example of God incrementally conforming to His own standard.

questions.question: Why does Valley Scharping argue that the divorce-concession argument 'proves too much' and 'proves too little'?
questions.audience: Skeptics who think abolitionists are merely splitting hairs over tactics
questions.answer: It proves too much because every Old-to-New Testament abrogation could then be relabeled incrementalism, justifying any 'increment' toward a good end. It proves too little because abolitionists do not object to all increments, only to those requiring moral compromise, and God never morally compromises.
questions.quote:
> 3. IT PROVES TOO MUCH. Every “abrogation” from the Old Testament to the New could be seen as incrementalism in this view, when that is clearly not the case. It could also be used to justify any and every “increment” toward any good end. But every Christian agrees that not all increments are morally acceptable.
>
> 4. IT PROVES TOO LITTLE. Because our problem isn’t with all “increments,” per se, but increments that require moral compromise. God never morally compromises, so even if this is an example of God conceding increments, it does not by any means prove that God was willing to morally compromise incrementally in order to achieve an end.

questions.question: How does Scharping reframe Mosaic divorce law as an 'increment of mercy' rather than an increment of tolerance?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
questions.answer: He argues God's temporary forbearance through Moses was a merciful refusal to crush His people under the full weight of His infinite moral law, not a pragmatic step toward eventual abolition of divorce. A heartbeat bill, by contrast, is man bargaining with murderers by trading lives, not God mercifully forbearing.
questions.quote: > 6. NOT AN INCREMENT OF TOLERANCE, BUT AN “INCREMENT” OF MERCY. Every moment God does not immediately punish all wickedness could be labelled incrementalism in the same vein, but God forebears in mercy and wisdom. Similarly, God through His Law temporarily forebearing for our sake through Moses is His merciful choice to not bear upon us the impossible weight of God’s infinite moral Law; it’s not a pragmatic step to full abolition of divorce. Are we to believe a 22-week ban written by man is given as a temporary mercy to not impose the infinite moral Law, and not as a pragmatic step to abolition?

questions.question: Why does Matthew 19 actually undermine, rather than support, the incrementalist reading of divorce law?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists preparing to engage smashmouth-incrementalist arguments
questions.answer: Jesus does not treat the Mosaic concession as a step toward a more perfect future law; He repeals the later concession and points back to the original creation ordinance. The passage is therefore fundamentally immediatist, not incrementalist.
questions.quote: > 7. NOT AN INCREMENT OF PROGRESS, BUT AN “INCREMENT” OF REGRESSION. The beginning of Matthew 19 makes clear that Jesus was not accumulating the law on divorce to a final, more perfect state. He says, “it was not so in the beginning.” ... He repeals a former increment to honor the first and final and only acceptable law on divorce. The passage is fundamentally immediatist.

questions.question: How does the article say Sumpter's 'two sons' parable principle should actually be applied?
questions.audience: Pro-life legislators weighing whether to support heartbeat or gestational-limit bills
questions.answer: The principle of preferring delayed obedience to ongoing refusal applies to inviting anti-abolitionist politicians to repent and join the abolitionist movement, not to celebrating incremental bills produced by pro-life politicians who continue to refuse God's law.
questions.quote: A correct application of this principle would be inviting anti-abolitionist politicians like Greg Treat, Jeff Leach, and Brent Crane — who have fought to keep abortion legal — to join the Abolitionist Movement, and to celebrate them when they do. An incorrect application of this principle is supporting and celebrating the idolatrous, unjust laws passed by pro-life politicians who perpetually refuse to obey God’s law.

questions.question: Do incremental bills really 'permit' abortion, as Sumpter's sixth tenet denies?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists preparing to engage smashmouth-incrementalist arguments
questions.answer: Yes. While they avoid the phrase 'kill a baby,' they specify when, where, and how abortions can lawfully occur, sometimes explicitly listing permitted instances and otherwise implicitly allowing the practice by regulating, e.g., abortion-mill hallway widths instead of criminalizing the murder.
questions.quote: Pro-life incremental bills, though they do not say “kill a baby” in that exact phraseology but are written in the preferred euphemisms of pro-aborts, do say when, where, and how you can kill a baby. Some of them explicitly list the instances where you CAN lawfully murder a child, and all of them implicitly allow abortions to take place by failing to outlaw the practice outright, such as laws that regulate the width of the hallways of abortion mills instead of criminalizing those who murder and those who hire assassins to murder.

questions.question: Why is the polygamy analogy a category error when applied to abortion legislation?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
questions.answer: God, as the lawgiver, may show mercy on lower-order sins like polygamy in ways man cannot. Incrementalism is not even framed in terms of mercy by Sumpter himself, and the laws meant to protect second wives are simply not analogous to laws that allow children to be murdered.
questions.quote: The analogy of divorce and polygamy fail for the same reason: God has a right to be merciful. Incrementalism is not about mercy even in the mind of Sumpter. He does not wish to allow a concession for people who murder children, he aims to save as many children as possible. There is simply NO ACCURATE ANALOGY between laws that are meant to protect second wives and laws that allow the murder of children.

questions.question: What is the authors' actual definition of incrementalism, and how do they distinguish it from immediatism?
questions.audience: Christians new to the abolitionism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: Incrementalism is policy advocacy of social change by degrees, meaning support for laws that compromise. Calling for the immediate abolition of abortion in a given jurisdiction is immediatism, even if abolition does not happen everywhere at once. The distinction is between obedience and disobedience to Christ in legislation.
questions.quote:
The man calling only for the immediate abolition of abortion in a given jurisdiction is an immediatist. The man calling for incremental steps toward the abolition of abortion is an incrementalist. It’s as simple as that.

Abolitionism is repentance and obedience. ... Regulating abortion (locally or nationally) is writing a law in rebellion to God’s Word. This is disobedience to Christ.

questions.question: How does the article respond to Sumpter's eighth-tenet appeal to the diversity of the body of Christ?
questions.audience: Skeptics who think abolitionists are merely splitting hairs over tactics
questions.answer: It concedes that not every Christian must serve at an abortion mill, but insists that everyone can call legislators, attend rallies, and advocate for their preborn neighbors. The 'different parts of the body' principle does not excuse the widespread abortion apathy in the American church.
questions.quote: It can be conceded that not everyone must go to an abortion facility or is particularly gifted in that area, but everyone can make phone calls. Everyone can attend rallies. Everyone can meet with their legislator. Everyone can advocate for their preborn neighbors to their friends and family.

questions.question: What scriptural warning does the article apply to churches that worship faithfully while ignoring child sacrifice?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and elders sympathetic to Sumpter, Wilson, and Cross Politic
questions.answer: It applies Isaiah 1 and Amos 5, where God says He will not listen to prayers, accept offerings, or endure assemblies from a people whose hands are full of blood. The remedy God prescribes is to seek justice and correct oppression, not to expand mercy ministry while abortion continues.
questions.quote: > “When you spread out your hands, I will hide my eyes from you; even though you make many prayers, I will not listen; your hands are full of blood. Wash yourselves; make yourselves clean; remove the evil of your deeds from before my eyes; cease to do evil, learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow’s cause.” – Isaiah 1:12-17

questions.question: Why can no Christian plead ignorance about the duty to oppose abortion, according to the article?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists preparing to engage smashmouth-incrementalist arguments
questions.answer: Because Leviticus 20:4-5 holds people accountable for failing to act against known child sacrifice, and Proverbs 24:12 anticipates and rejects the excuse 'we did not know.' God weighs the heart and repays men for what they did and did not do.
questions.quote:
> “If you say, “Behold, we did not know this,” does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who keeps watch over your soul know it, and will he not repay man according to his work?

We all know that abortion is happening among us. We cannot hide behind the excuse of ignorance. God holds men accountable for what they do and what they don’t do regarding the killing of our neighbors.

related.title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
related.reason: Explicitly cited in the article as the rebuttal to Sumpter's claim that Scripture praises incrementalist kings.

related.title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
related.reason: Linked in the article as part of the broader catalog of refutations of Wilson and Sumpter's smashmouth-incrementalism camp.

related.title: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
related.reason: Cited in the article as a companion piece showing how a Reformed thinker sympathetic to error nevertheless rejected gradualism in principle.

related.title: Dang it, Doug.
related.reason: Same author's prior salvo against Doug Wilson's incrementalism, referenced directly in the article.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Provides the concise definition of the two positions that this article relies on when correcting Sumpter's equivocation in Tenet 7.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the theological case that compromise legislation is idolatry, the very claim Sumpter conceded on-air with Jeff Durbin.

related.title: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
related.reason: Companion piece showing the same equivocations and category errors among other defenders of incremental abortion legislation.
